items.question_text: TERMINAL: When a STAR/RNAV STAR transition is designed to provide course guidance to multiple runways, when must the runway number be stated?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-1b: The facility must state intended runway number on initial contact, or as soon as practical. If runway assignment is not issued prior to 10 NM from the runway transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

choices.text: After the aircraft has passed the transition waypoint.
On initial contact or as soon as practical; if not by 10 NM from runway transition waypoint, provide radar vectors to final.
Only after the pilot requests the runway assignment.
When the aircraft reaches the IAF.

items.question_text: When a STAR/RNAV STAR transition change is made for a runway, what is the correct phraseology?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-1b: 'CHANGE/AMEND TRANSITION TO (runway number).' If vector to final needed: 'CHANGE/AMEND TRANSITION TO (runway number) TURN LEFT/RIGHT or HEADING (heading) FOR VECTOR TO FINAL APPROACH COURSE.'

choices.text: 'CHANGE RUNWAY TO (runway).'
'CHANGE/AMEND TRANSITION TO (runway number).'
'NEW RUNWAY ASSIGNMENT (runway).'
'REVISED ARRIVAL, RUNWAY (runway).'

items.question_text: EN ROUTE: When controlling aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the common boundary, what two actions must be taken?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-2: (a) Coordinate with the receiving facility for a lower altitude and issue clearance as appropriate. (b) Initiate this action at a distance sufficient from destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: Issue holding and wait for the adjacent center to accept the handoff.
Coordinate lower altitude with receiving facility and initiate early enough for normal descent/speed reduction.
Transfer communications and let the receiving facility issue all clearances.
Clear the aircraft to the IAF at the last assigned altitude.

items.question_text: For SFA procedures with military single-piloted turbojet aircraft, a frequency change after approach begins is permitted under which conditions?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-3: Frequency change permitted during: (a) daylight VFR, (b) pilot request, (c) pilot cancels IFR, (d) emergency, or (e) visual approach clearance.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft cancels IFR.
During daylight VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, emergency, or visual approach.
Any time the controller deems necessary.
Only in an emergency or on pilot request.

items.question_text: For military single-piloted turbojet aircraft on approach in IFR or at night, below what altitude should frequency/beacon changes be kept to a minimum?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-4a2: Keep frequency/radar beacon changes to a minimum below 2,500 feet above the surface.

choices.text: Below 1,000 feet AGL.
Below 2,500 feet above the surface.
Below 5,000 feet MSL.
Below 10,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: For military single-piloted turbojet aircraft, when should frequency/beacon changes be avoided specifically?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-4a3: Avoid requiring frequency/radar beacon changes during the time the aircraft is making a turn.

choices.text: During the final approach segment.
During the time the aircraft is making a turn.
During climb out.
During level flight above 10,000 feet.

items.question_text: When may a military aircraft making a nonradar approach be instructed to change to tower frequency?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-4e: When (1) ceiling >= 1,500 ft and vis >= 5 SM, or (2) aircraft reports able to proceed visually, or (3) contact approach requested and cleared, or (4) visual approach cleared.

choices.text: Only after the aircraft reports the field in sight.
When ceiling >= 1,500 ft and vis >= 5 SM, or aircraft reports visual, or contact/visual approach cleared.
At any time after the final approach fix.
Only on pilot request.

items.question_text: For military turbojet en route descent, how is the distance from destination to initiate descent determined?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-5d: Add 10 to the first two digits of the flight level. Example: FL 370 -> 37 + 10 = 47 miles.

choices.text: Multiply flight level by 3 for miles.
Add 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.
Subtract 5 from the flight level divided by 10.
Use a constant 50 miles for all altitudes.

items.question_text: Prior to issuing a descent clearance below the highest initial approach fix altitude, what must a military turbojet aircraft be informed of?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-5b: Inform the aircraft of: (1) type of approach to expect, (2) radar vectors will be provided to final approach course, and (3) current weather when ceiling < 1,000 ft (USAF: 1,500 ft) or visibility < 3 miles.

choices.text: Only the approach type to expect.
Approach type, radar vectors to final, and weather when below 1,000 ft ceiling (USAF 1,500 ft) or < 3 mi vis.
Approach type, holding instructions, and EFC.
Only the runway in use and weather.

items.question_text: When may an en route descent of a military turbojet be terminated without pilot consent?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-5e: Do not terminate en route descent without pilot consent except as required by radar outage or an emergency situation.

choices.text: When the aircraft reaches the MVA.
Only when required by radar outage or an emergency situation.
When the aircraft enters the approach control area.
At any time the controller deems necessary.

items.question_text: EN ROUTE: What information must be forwarded to approach control facilities before transfer of jurisdiction?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-6b: Forward: (1) aircraft ID, (2) type/suffix, (3) ETA or actual time + altitude over clearance limit, (4) clearance limit (if not destination) and EFC, (5) time/fix/altitude when control transfers.

choices.text: Only aircraft ID and ETA.
Aircraft ID, type/suffix, ETA/altitude, clearance limit/EFC, and transfer control point.
Aircraft ID, destination, and STAR assignment.
Only the information required for a radar handoff.

items.question_text: EN ROUTE: When must a weather report be transmitted to an arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-7: When weather is below 1,000-foot ceiling (USAF: 1,500 ft) or below highest circling minimum, whichever is higher, or less than 3 miles visibility.

choices.text: When the ceiling is below 500 feet or visibility below 1 mile.
When ceiling < 1,000 ft (USAF 1,500 ft) or below highest circling minimum (whichever higher), or vis < 3 mi.
Only for military aircraft.
Only when the weather is below landing minima.

items.question_text: If an arriving aircraft reports weather conditions are below landing minima, what two actions should be taken?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-8: (a) Issue instructions to hold or proceed to another airport. (b) Adjust position in landing sequence of other aircraft and issue approach clearances accordingly.

choices.text: Cancel the IFR flight plan and clear the aircraft to an alternate.
Issue hold or alternate airport instructions, and adjust other aircraft's landing sequence.
Inform the pilot to attempt the approach at their discretion.
Close the airport to all arriving traffic.

items.question_text: When must controllers issue ceiling and visibility as part of approach information?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-10a4: Issue ceiling and visibility if reported ceiling at airport is below 1,000 feet or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater, or the visibility is less than 3 miles.

choices.text: Only when below published landing minima.
When ceiling < 1,000 ft or below highest circling minimum (whichever greater), or visibility < 3 miles.
For all instrument approaches regardless of conditions.
Only when the pilot requests it.

items.question_text: What must controllers do when the ILS on the runway in use is not operational but another ILS is on the same frequency?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-10d: Advise pilots when the ILS on the runway in use is not operational if that ILS is on the same frequency as an operational ILS serving another runway.

choices.text: Switch all aircraft to the operational ILS runway.
Advise pilots that the runway's ILS is not operational when it shares frequency with an operational ILS.
Disable both ILS systems until the situation is resolved.
No advisory is needed since pilots can identify the correct ILS by Morse code.

items.question_text: When must RwyCC contained in a FICON NOTAM be issued to aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-12c: Issue RwyCC: (1) before/upon approach clearance, (2) before en route descent clearance, (3) TERMINAL: prior to departure, (4) as soon as possible after subsequent changes.

choices.text: Only when the pilot requests it.
Before/upon approach clearance, before en route descent, prior to departure (terminal), and after changes.
Only on the ATIS broadcast.
Only when runway conditions are below good.

items.question_text: Can a controller issue RwyCC in lieu of the complete FICON NOTAM?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-12d: RwyCC may be issued in lieu of the complete FICON NOTAM. Issue the complete FICON NOTAM upon pilot request, workload permitting.

choices.text: No, the complete FICON NOTAM must always be read.
Yes, RwyCC may be issued in lieu of complete FICON NOTAM; issue complete on pilot request.
Only at uncontrolled airports.
Only when the FICON is more than 1 hour old.
